intro: Converting logic from C++ to SQL transforms code from an imperative, step-by-step memory manipulation application into a declarative, set-based database execution model. Instead of designing manual iterators and algorithms to slice arrays, SQL describes *what* data subset is needed and lets the database optimizer execute the extraction. This transition drastically reduces latency for data-heavy tasks by executing operations directly where the data resides.

diffs.from: Static, object-oriented system built around class compositions, pointer addresses, and strict template variables.
diffs.to: Relational, schema-bound types specifying discrete tabular columns, constraints, null states, and explicit primary keys.

diffs.from: Imperative structure utilizing explicit assignment expressions, scoped conditional branching loops, and object declarations.
diffs.to: Declarative syntax using uniform query clauses (SELECT, JOIN, WHERE, GROUP BY) acting entirely on rows and relations.

diffs.from: Granular control over stack frame scopes, heap segment structures, allocation pools, and raw memory addresses.
diffs.to: Abstracted completely by the database engine buffer manager, which optimizes memory caching, indexes, and execution plans.

diffs.from: Thread abstractions, lock primitives, memory boundaries, and asynchronous callback loops.
diffs.to: Implicit parallel query execution engines, isolated completely via transactional ACID properties and locking levels.

diffs.from: Containers, computational sorting, string tools, and operating system IO utilities.
diffs.to: Aggregations (SUM, AVG, COUNT), window functions, table joins, and transactional database isolation controls.

examples.title: Conditional Collection Aggregation
examples.description: Compares manual filtration and calculation over an object loop in C++ against a declarative SQL aggregate grouping.

examples.title: Inner Equi-Join Equivalent
examples.description: Shows mapping two separate object groups via nested hash iteration in C++ versus a standardized relational SQL JOIN statement.

tips.title: Adopt Set-Based Thinking
tips.body: Never attempt to emulate C++ loops using SQL Cursors unless absolutely necessary. Cursors execute line-by-line and slow down relational engines; instead, use JOINs and window functions to process blocks uniformly.

tips.title: Offload Constraints to Schema
tips.body: Replace C++ validation rules with relational database constraints like `FOREIGN KEY`, `UNIQUE`, `CHECK`, and `NOT NULL` to ensure absolute structural safety directly at the persistence layer.

tips.title: Leverage Database Indexes
tips.body: In C++, lookups depend on picking the right container (like `std::unordered_map`). In SQL, ensure columns utilized frequently in `WHERE` and `JOIN` expressions are explicitly indexed to maintain rapid performance.

tips.title: Use Stored Procedures for Logic
tips.body: For complex structural algorithms, write Stored Procedures or Common Table Expressions (CTEs) to execute complex data transformations inside the database without incurring network round-trip overhead.
